exercises.title: The cold-start tax
exercises.prompt: Every session that starts cold pays a tax. Explain the cold-start tax (the work an agent redoes per session), and identify the work an agent should never have to redo. Why does an init script pay this tax once?
exercises.hint: Cold start: each session re-reads the same files, re-probes runtime/deps, rediscovers paths/commands — wasted tokens and time, every session. Never-redo work: deterministic environment facts (runtime version, deps installed, test command, repo structure). Init script: runs the probes ONCE, writes answers to state, agent reads instead of re-probing.
exercises.solution:
**The cold-start tax:** **every session that starts cold pays a tax** — it **redoes the same orientation work** that the previous session already did:
- **Re-reads the same files** to understand the repo structure.
- **Re-probes the runtime** (what Python/Node version? is the environment set up?).
- **Re-checks dependencies** (are packages installed? what's available?).
- **Rediscovers paths and commands** (where's the entry point? how do I run the tests? what's the build command?).
- **Re-runs the same health checks** (does the repo even build/test?).
This is **wasted tokens, time, and tool calls — *every single session*.** The agent rediscovers the same deterministic facts repeatedly because **cold sessions have no memory of the prior orientation** (each starts fresh). The cold-start tax is **redundant, repeated work** that adds latency and cost to every session's startup.

**The work an agent should never have to redo:** **deterministic environment/repo facts that don't change between sessions:**
- **Runtime facts:** the language/runtime version, the environment setup, available tools.
- **Dependency facts:** what's installed, what's available, dependency versions.
- **Repo structure facts:** the entry point, key directories, the project layout.
- **Command facts:** how to run tests, build, lint, start the app (the test command, build command).
- **Repo health:** does it build/test cleanly (the baseline)?
These are **deterministic probes** — the answer is the **same every session** (until the environment actually changes). The agent should **never re-derive them per session** — they should be **probed once and recorded.** (Contrast: the *task* changes per session — that's not the cold-start tax; the tax is the *constant environment facts* being rediscovered.)

**Why an init script pays this tax once:** an **init script** is a **deterministic script that probes the environment/repo and writes the answers into state** — paying the cold-start tax **once** and **recording the results** so the agent reads them instead of re-probing:
- **Run the probes once:** the init script **deterministically probes** runtime, dependencies, repo structure, commands, and health — doing the orientation work **once** (not per session).
- **Persist the answers to state (Lesson 34):** it **writes the probe results into the durable state file** — 'Python 3.11, deps installed, test command = pytest, entry point = src/main.py, repo builds cleanly.'
- **The agent reads instead of re-probing:** subsequent sessions **read the recorded answers from state** (cheap — just reading a file) **instead of re-running the probes** (expensive — tool calls, tokens, time). The agent gets the environment facts **instantly from state**, not by rediscovery.
So the init script **converts repeated expensive rediscovery (cold-start tax, every session) into a one-time probe + cheap reads (pay once, read forever).** It **pays the tax once and writes the answers down** — eliminating the redundant per-session orientation work. This is the same principle as **caching** (Phase 11.11 — compute once, reuse) and **prompt caching** (Phase 11.15 — process the stable prefix once) applied to **session orientation**: **the deterministic environment facts are computed once (init script) and cached in state (Lesson 34), so every session reads them instead of recomputing.** It makes sessions **start fast and cheap** (read the recorded facts) instead of paying the cold-start tax repeatedly.

exercises.title: Building a deterministic init script
exercises.prompt: Build a deterministic init script that probes runtime, dependencies, and repo health, and persists the result to state. Explain why determinism matters, what to probe, and how the agent uses the persisted result. When should the init script re-run vs use cached results?
exercises.hint: Probe: runtime version, deps installed, test/build commands work, repo structure. Persist to state (Lesson 34). Deterministic so the same environment gives the same result (reproducible, cacheable). Agent reads the persisted probe. Re-run when the environment changes (deps updated, new session in a changed repo); cache otherwise.
exercises.solution:
```python
import subprocess, json

def init_probe(repo_path):
    """Deterministically probe the environment/repo, return structured facts."""
    result = {
        "runtime": probe_runtime(),                  # e.g. python --version
        "deps_installed": probe_deps(repo_path),      # are deps present?
        "test_command": detect_test_command(repo_path),  # pytest? npm test?
        "build_command": detect_build_command(repo_path),
        "entry_point": detect_entry_point(repo_path),
        "repo_structure": map_key_dirs(repo_path),
        "health": run_health_check(repo_path),        # does it build/test cleanly?
        "probed_at": now(), "repo_hash": git_head(repo_path),  # for cache validity
    }
    return result

def run_init(repo_path, state_manager):
    facts = init_probe(repo_path)
    state_manager.mutate(lambda s: s.update({"environment": facts}))  # persist (L34)
    return facts
# Subsequent sessions: agent reads state["environment"] instead of re-probing.
```
The init script **probes the environment deterministically and persists the facts to state** (via the state manager, Lesson 34).

**What to probe:**
- **Runtime:** language/runtime version, environment setup (is it activated/configured?).
- **Dependencies:** are deps installed? versions? (so the agent doesn't discover missing deps mid-task).
- **Commands:** the **test command, build command, lint command** (detected from the repo — package.json, pyproject, Makefile) — so the agent knows how to run things.
- **Repo structure:** entry point, key directories, project layout.
- **Health:** **does the repo build/test cleanly** (the baseline)? — so the agent knows the starting state (and a later failure isn't blamed on the agent if the repo was already broken).
- **Cache-validity metadata:** `probed_at` timestamp and `repo_hash` (git HEAD) — to decide when to re-probe (below).

**Why determinism matters:** the init script must be **deterministic** (same environment → same result) because:
- **Reproducibility:** a deterministic probe gives **consistent, trustworthy facts** — every session reads the same correct facts (no flaky/varying probe results that confuse the agent).
- **Cacheability:** **only deterministic results can be safely cached** — if the probe were non-deterministic, the cached facts might be wrong (you couldn't trust the recorded answer). Determinism is **what makes the 'pay once, read forever' caching valid** (Exercise 1) — the cached facts are reliably correct because the probe is deterministic.
- **Debuggability:** a deterministic init script is **testable and predictable** (run it, get the known result) — vs a non-deterministic one that's hard to reason about.
Determinism is essential because the whole point is to **cache the result** — and you can only safely cache a **deterministic** computation (Phase 11.11's caching, Lesson 1's deterministic-executor principle).

**How the agent uses the persisted result:** subsequent sessions **read the `environment` facts from state** (Lesson 34) at startup — getting the **runtime, commands, structure, and health instantly** — instead of re-probing. The agent **uses the recorded test command** to run tests, the **recorded entry point** to navigate, the **recorded health** to know the baseline — all **read from state, not rediscovered.** This is the read-at-start orientation (Lesson 32) **powered by the init script's recorded facts** — the agent orients instantly from the cached probe.

**When to re-run vs use cached results:** 
- **Use cached results** when the **environment hasn't changed** — the common case. The agent reads the persisted facts (cheap). This is the win (avoid the cold-start tax).
- **Re-run the init script when the environment *has* changed** — invalidate the cache when:
  - **Dependencies changed** (a package was installed/updated — the deps facts are stale).
  - **The repo changed significantly** (a new commit that changed structure/commands — check via the `repo_hash`/git HEAD vs the recorded one).
  - **The cache is too old** (a TTL — re-probe periodically to catch drift).
  - **A probed fact turns out wrong** (a recorded command fails — re-probe to refresh).
The **cache-validity metadata (`probed_at`, `repo_hash`)** lets the agent **decide**: 'is the cached environment still valid (same repo hash, recent, deps unchanged)? → use it. Has it changed? → re-run init.' This is **cache invalidation** (Phase 11.11) applied to the init probe — **use the cache while valid, re-probe when the environment changes.** The discipline: **probe once, cache the facts with validity metadata, read the cache when valid, re-probe on change** — paying the cold-start tax once and refreshing only when the environment actually changes. This keeps sessions fast (read cached facts) while staying correct (re-probe on change) — the init script's core value.

exercises.title: Init scripts in the workbench, determinism vs the agent, and the broader 'do work once' principle
exercises.prompt: Explain how init scripts fit the workbench (feeding state, enabling fast orientation), why deterministic scripts should do deterministic work (not the LLM), the relationship to runtime feedback (Lesson 37), and the broader principle: push deterministic work to scripts, reserve the LLM for judgment. What are the limits?
exercises.hint: Init scripts feed durable state (Lesson 34) so the agent orients fast (Lesson 32). Deterministic work → a script (reproducible, cheap, reliable), not the LLM (expensive, non-deterministic, can hallucinate). Relates to feedback (both capture real facts into state). Principle: scripts for deterministic facts, LLM for judgment. Limits: not everything is scriptable; env can change.
exercises.solution:
**How init scripts fit the workbench:** init scripts are the **orientation-automation surface** that feeds the rest of the workbench:
- **They feed durable state (Lesson 34):** the init script **writes environment facts into the state file** — so the durable state (the workbench's source of truth) includes the **probed environment** (runtime, commands, structure, health). The state manager persists it; subsequent sessions read it.
- **They enable fast orientation (Lesson 32):** the agent's read-at-start orientation (Lesson 32) **reads the init script's recorded facts** — so the agent orients **instantly** (read the cached environment) instead of paying the cold-start tax (re-probing). Init scripts make the read-at-start orientation **fast and complete.**
- **They establish the baseline for verification (Lesson 38):** the init script's **health check** records whether the repo was clean *before* the agent worked — so the verification gate (Lesson 38) can distinguish 'the agent broke it' from 'it was already broken.'
Init scripts are the **'pay the orientation tax once, feed state' surface** — they make every session start oriented (from cached facts) rather than cold.

**Why deterministic scripts should do deterministic work (not the LLM):** the init script does **deterministic probing** (run `python --version`, check if deps are installed, detect the test command) — and this work **should be a script, NOT the LLM:**
- **Deterministic work → deterministic tool (a script):** probing the environment is a **deterministic computation** (the answer is fixed by the environment). A **script does it reliably, reproducibly, cheaply, and correctly** — `subprocess.run('python --version')` always gives the right answer. 
- **The LLM is the wrong tool for deterministic work:** if you made the **LLM probe the environment** (reason about what version, guess the test command), it would be **expensive (LLM calls), non-deterministic (varying results), and could *hallucinate*** (guess the wrong command, assume deps that aren't there — hallucinated actions, Lesson 26). The LLM is **slower, costlier, and less reliable** for work that has a **deterministic, computable answer.**
So: **deterministic facts → a deterministic script (reliable, cheap, correct); the LLM should never re-derive what a script can compute.** This is the core principle: **don't use the expensive, non-deterministic LLM for work a cheap, deterministic script can do reliably.**

**Relationship to runtime feedback (Lesson 37):** init scripts and runtime feedback (Lesson 37) are **closely related — both capture *real facts* into state for the agent to read (rather than the agent guessing):**
- **Init scripts:** capture **deterministic *environment* facts once at startup** (runtime, commands, structure) → state.
- **Runtime feedback (Lesson 37):** captures **real *command-execution* facts during the run** (actual stdout/stderr/exit codes of commands the agent runs) → state.
Both follow the same principle: **capture real, deterministic facts into state so the agent reacts to *facts*, not its *predictions* of facts** (Lesson 37's thesis — 'agents that don't see real output guess'). Init scripts capture the **static environment facts** (probed once); runtime feedback captures the **dynamic execution facts** (captured per command). Together they **ground the agent in reality** — the init script for the environment, runtime feedback for command results — both **feeding real facts into state** so the agent doesn't hallucinate the environment or assume command outcomes. They're two facets of 'ground the agent in real, captured facts, not guesses.'

**The broader principle (push deterministic work to scripts, reserve the LLM for judgment):** this is a **central agent-engineering principle**:
- **Deterministic work → scripts:** anything with a **deterministic, computable answer** (environment probes, running tests, checking file existence, validating schemas, computing diffs, applying rules — Lessons 33, 37, 38) should be done by a **deterministic script** — **reliable, cheap, reproducible, no hallucination.**
- **Judgment → the LLM:** reserve the **expensive, non-deterministic LLM for what genuinely requires judgment/reasoning** — understanding the task, deciding the approach, writing the code, reviewing for quality (Lesson 39). The LLM does the **irreducibly-judgment work**; scripts do the **deterministic work.**
**Why:** using the LLM for deterministic work is **wasteful (cost), unreliable (non-determinism, hallucination), and unnecessary** (a script does it better). Using scripts for judgment is impossible (scripts can't reason). So **partition the work: deterministic → scripts, judgment → LLM** — this makes the agent **cheaper, more reliable, and more deterministic** (the deterministic parts are *actually* deterministic). The whole workbench embodies this: **init scripts (deterministic probing), rule checkers (deterministic constraint-checking, Lesson 33), feedback runners (deterministic command capture, Lesson 37), verification gates (deterministic checks, Lesson 38) are all *scripts*** — and the **LLM does the judgment** (the actual building, reviewing). Pushing deterministic work to scripts is what makes the workbench **reliable** (the deterministic parts can't hallucinate or vary) — a key reliability lever.

**The limits:**
- **Not everything is scriptable:** some 'facts' require **judgment** (is the code well-designed? is this the right approach?) — those **can't be a deterministic script** (they need the LLM/reviewer, Lesson 39). The script handles the **objectively computable**; the LLM handles the rest.
- **The environment can change:** init facts go **stale** when the environment changes (deps update, repo changes) — so you need **cache invalidation** (Exercise 2 — re-probe on change). A cached-but-stale environment fact is a failure mode (the agent acts on outdated facts).
- **Probes can be incomplete:** the init script only captures **what it's coded to probe** — it might miss something the agent needs (then the agent must discover it, partially re-incurring the tax). You iterate the init script to cover more.
- **Init scripts add maintenance** — they're code to maintain as the repo/environment evolves.
The synthesis: **init scripts pay the cold-start tax once** — deterministically probing the environment and **caching the facts in state (Lesson 34)** so every session orients **fast and cheap** (read cached facts, Lesson 32) instead of re-discovering them. They embody the **'deterministic work → scripts, judgment → LLM'** principle (reliable/cheap probing by a script, not expensive/hallucination-prone LLM rediscovery), relate to **runtime feedback** (both capture real facts into state, grounding the agent), and are **invalidated/re-run when the environment changes** (Exercise 2). The broader lesson — **push deterministic work to scripts, reserve the LLM for judgment** — runs through the whole workbench (init, rule-checking, feedback, verification are all deterministic scripts) and is a key reliability lever: **the deterministic parts of an agent system should be *actually* deterministic (scripts), not hopefully-deterministic (the LLM).** Init scripts make session orientation a one-time deterministic computation rather than a repeated LLM rediscovery — fast, cheap, reliable startup.
